title: Experimental Evidence on Negative Impact of Generative AI on Scientific Learning Outcomes (A Pilot Research)
study_design_description: This pilot experimental study took place from August 20th to 29th, 2023, with 32 college-educated participants recruited from Prolific. Each participant underwent three rounds of tasks involving reading a paper, crafting a summary based on a given prompt, and answering five multiple-choice questions. Participants were incentivized with a 30% bonus payment if they answered 80% of questions accurately. The order of papers within each group was randomized to prevent fatigue effects. This pre-registered experiment follows the protocol at Duke Institutional Review Board.
primary_task_description: Each participant underwent three rounds of tasks which entailed reading a paper, crafting a summary based on a given prompt, and answering five questions pertinent to the paper. For each segment of the experiment, participants were given a maximum of 10 minutes to read the article and complete the writing task. After reading each paper, participants had five minutes to answer five multiple-choice questions about the content. The writing tasks fell into three categories: 1) Manual writing of summary reports without AI support (Manual), 2) Using AI for assistance in writing the summary report (AI), and 3) Individualized guided active reading through chat (Active). In the third category, participants weren't mandated to write a summary but could opt to use AI to enhance their comprehension by asking questions.
population_description: A total of 32 college-educated individuals were recruited from the online platform Prolific, evenly split with 16 males and 16 females. These participants represented a wide spectrum of academic backgrounds, ranging from humanities and social sciences to natural sciences, medicine, and engineering. Prior to the study, participants were screened to verify their access to and understanding of Generative AI tools. Age Average: 32.41. Highest Degree: Bachelor 66.67%, Master 22.22%, Doctoral (Including JD or MD) 8.89%, Associate 2.22%. English Native: Yes 46.67%, No 53.33%. Employment status: Full-Time 55.88%, Unemployed (and job seeking) 20.59%, Part-Time 14.71%, Due to start a new job within the next month 5.88%, Other 2.94%.

arms.description: Manual writing of summary reports without AI support. For the manual writing task, participants were prohibited from copying and pasting from the reading material and writing boxes, ensuring they didn't utilize AI.
Using AI for assistance in writing the summary report.
Individualized guided active reading through chat. In this category, participants weren't mandated to write a summary but could opt to use AI to enhance their comprehension by asking questions.

outcomes.outcome_description: Accuracy of answers to five multiple-choice questions about the paper content.
Quality of summary report evaluated on comprehensiveness, scored from 1 to 5.
Quality of summary report evaluated on grammar, scored from 1 to 5.
Quality of summary report evaluated on flow, scored from 1 to 5.
Overall grade determined by averaging scores across comprehensiveness, grammar, and flow dimensions.

reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: After adjusting for background knowledge and demographic factors, complete reliance on AI for writing tasks led to a 25.1% reduction in accuracy. In contrast, AI-assisted reading resulted in a 12% decline. In the study, participants who tackled writing assignments without any external assistance achieved an average grade of 66.7%. Conversely, those fully reliant on AI for crafting their assignments scored an average of 48.4%. The observed differences are statistically significant at the 5% level, with p-values of 0.002459 between the AI and Manual groups, and 0.005081 between the Active and Manual groups.

After adjusting for background knowledge and demographic factors, complete reliance on AI for writing tasks led to a 25.1% reduction in accuracy. In contrast, AI-assisted reading resulted in a 12% decline. In the study, participants who tackled writing assignments without any external assistance achieved an average grade of 66.7%. Conversely, those fully reliant on AI for crafting their assignments scored an average of 48.4%. The "Active" group, which utilized AI to aid their comprehension of the paper, secured an average of 48.9% and exhibited the most diverse grade distribution, as showcased in Figure 1. The observed differences are statistically significant at the 5% level, with p-values of 0.002459 between the AI and Manual groups, and 0.005081 between the Active and Manual groups.
The standard deviations for the Active, AI, and Manual groups are 0.3178209, 0.287588, and 0.266287, respectively.

notes: This is a pilot study with 32 participants (n not specified per arm; total n=32 with 3 rounds per participant = 96 observations, but the statistical results table shows 135 observations for correctness, suggesting some data structure complexity). The study compares three within-subject conditions: manual writing (control), AI-assisted writing, and active reading with AI. Key findings: complete reliance on AI reduced accuracy by 25.1% (p=0.002459), while partial reliance (active reading) reduced accuracy by 12% (p=0.005081), both after adjusting for background knowledge and demographics. The paper also reports that AI-assisted writing produced higher-quality output (fewer spelling errors, better flow, coverage) but participants completed tasks faster (8.7-9 minutes vs. nearly full 10 minutes for manual). The Active group showed bimodal distribution of results, indicating heterogeneous effects. Limitations noted: results may not generalize beyond higher education; short 39-minute total duration may not capture long-term effects; focus limited to writing assistance only; different AI chatbots could produce different outcomes.
